mowers.tip: £5500–9000 used. Kubota diesel hours: under 1500 is excellent for a G23, under 3000 acceptable. Diesel injectors need clean fuel — ask about service history. Hydraulic system pressure test should be part of any pre-purchase inspection on a unit over £7000.

pros: Newer D902 diesel — cleaner-burning than D722
cons: Even more expensive than the G23
mowers.verdict: The current Kubota G-series flagship. Replaces the G21/G23 generation with cleaner emissions and an electronic dash. Same Kubota build quality and the same 25-30 year horizon if serviced. Domestic buyers stop reading at the price; estate owners and grounds contractors are the actual market.
mowers.tip: £6500–11000 used. New enough that hours under 1000 are common. D902 service intervals matter — confirm regular oil and filter changes. Electronic dash faults are the new failure mode — budget for a Kubota dealer diagnostic on any unit over 5 years old.

mowers.tagline: Kubota entry petrol ride-on — the smallest T-series
cons: Kubota dealer network thinner than JD
mowers.verdict: The Kubota for buyers who want Japanese build quality at a price closer to a premium John Deere X-series. Kawasaki engine is the win — properly built, parts widely available, runs forever. No collection is the trade-off.
mowers.tip: £2200–3600 used. Kawasaki FR541V hours under 500 excellent. Test the hydrostatic drive in both directions — must be smooth. Kubota service stamps add value. Side-discharge chute should pivot cleanly without binding.

mowers.tagline: Kubota sub-compact tractor with mid-mount mower deck
pros: Sub-compact tractor first, mower second — takes loaders, scrapers, snow blades
cons: Tractor first, mower second — mowing is one capability of many
Eye-watering price for a domestic mower
mowers.verdict: For people with smallholdings, large estates, or commercial groundskeeping needs. The BX1880 is a real tractor that happens to mow. If you also need to plough snow, move logs, or grade tracks, this is the answer. Pure-mowing buyers should look at the GR or T series.
mowers.tip: £5500–8000 used for mower-config. Kubota BX-series tractors hold value remarkably well. Confirm the mid-mount deck is included (often sold separately new). Hours under 1500 is typical for a domestic-use BX. Hydraulic system service history matters.

mowers.tagline: AP-system 41cm cordless — the Stihl trade-shop favourite
pros: Same AP battery as Stihl chainsaws and hedgetrimmers
Stihl dealer network — parts available everywhere
cons: AP 300 S batteries are expensive without a tool bundle
55L box is small for a 41cm deck
Front wheels feel cheap for the price
mowers.verdict: If you already own Stihl AP-system tools, this is the obvious cordless mower — one battery range, one charger, one shelf in the shed. Build quality is a step above the Bosch and Ryobi rivals at the same price, and the dealer support is what you actually pay extra for. Skip it if you're starting fresh on cordless; the EGO platform gives you more mower for the money.
mowers.tip: Skin price is £549 but you need the AP 300 S batteries (£189 each) and AL 301 charger (£89). Used kit-form RMA 443 TCs land at £280-350 with two used batteries — that is the buy. Check the battery cells: open Stihl service mode, and any cell pack reading under 80% capacity is worth £40 less.

pros: 21-inch steel deck — petrol-class cut width on a battery
Variable-speed self-drive via the bail-arm trigger
EGO 56V batteries are the largest on the market
Folds vertically for storage — fits any shed
cons: 32kg is heavy for a battery mower
5Ah battery only just covers 600m² — bigger lawns need 7.5Ah
Mulch plug is a separate buy
mowers.verdict: The mower that finally killed the small-petrol-self-propelled category. Cuts as wide as a Honda HRG, runs for 45 minutes on a 5Ah battery, and costs less to run than a Hyundai. If you have a 1000m² lawn and don't want petrol, this is the answer. The only competition is from EGO's own LMX5300SP-XP, which is the same machine with a brushless motor and a longer warranty — buy the LMX if it's only £80 more.
mowers.tip: Used LM2114E-SPs trade at £400-500 with a battery and charger. The deal is to find one ex-display on eBay with the battery still under EGO's 5-year warranty — register it in your name and you effectively get a new mower for half-price. Avoid any without the original charger; aftermarket EGO chargers are unreliable.

mowers.tagline: Cheap-and-cheerful 38cm cordless for small UK gardens
pros: Cheapest cordless from a recognised brand under £350
16kg — genuinely light, easy to carry up steps
Battery shared with Mountfield's hedgetrimmer and blower
cons: 24V is underpowered — bogs in long or wet grass
Plastic deck dents if you hit a stone
mowers.verdict: Built down to a price, but the price is the point. For a flat front lawn under 250m² that gets a weekly cut from April to October, the Princess 38 Li is enough mower. Don't expect it to handle three-week-old growth or anything resembling rough — it's a tidy-up tool, not a workhorse. The Greenworks G24X2LM41 is a better mower for £40 more.
mowers.tip: Used Princess 38 Lis flood the market every August at £140-180 from people who upgraded to a Flymo robot. Check the battery age — Mountfield batteries die at 3 years regardless of charge cycles. Replacement is £79 from STIGA spares; refuse to pay more than £150 if the battery is over 2 years old.

pros: Honda GXV — the engine that landscapers actually buy
BBC blade-brake clutch — engine stays running between cuts
cons: Lawnflite dealer network is patchy in the south-west
No roller — bowling-club lawns need to look elsewhere
mowers.verdict: MTD-built but specced like a small commercial mower — Honda GXV up top, alloy frame, BBC clutch, the lot. If you'd otherwise be eyeing a Hayter Harrier 56 or a Honda HRH 536, the Lawnflite Pro 553HRS comes in £400 cheaper and cuts the same width. The trade-off is resale value — Lawnflites don't hold money like Hondas or Hayters do.
mowers.tip: Used Pro 553HRSs are scarce because landscapers run them into the ground, but ex-demo and one-season returns occasionally appear at £550-700. The GXV engine has 1500+ hours in it before a top-end rebuild — buy on engine compression, not appearance. Anything with a deck rust spot is fine; that's just paint, not the alloy frame.

mowers.tagline: Mid-range NERA-platform robot — EPOS-ready, no boundary wire needed
pros: EPOS-compatible — wireless boundary if you add the reference station
Husqvarna Connect app with weather timer
cons: On boundary wire alone, the cheaper 305 does 80% of the job
Cutting heights only via app — no wheel
mowers.verdict: The 320 NERA is the Husqvarna model where wireless EPOS boundary actually starts to make sense — the smaller 305/310 NERA can do EPOS too but the lawn area is too small to justify the £900 reference station. For 1500-2200m² lawns with awkward shapes that you don't want to dig a wire into, this is the right Automower. For everything else the 305 NERA is two-thirds the price.
mowers.tip: Used 320 NERAs are starting to appear at £1300-1600 from people who downsized. NERA platform launched 2023 so battery health is still strong. Confirm the EPOS antenna is intact under the bonnet — replacement is £180 from Husqvarna. Always demand the original charging station; aftermarket ones don't trigger NERA's encrypted handshake.

mowers.tagline: AI-camera 1600m² robot — no boundary wire, no reference station
pros: True wire-free — AI camera sees grass vs. not-grass
1600m² is the biggest in the Vision range
Software updates over wifi — model gets smarter monthly
PowerShare battery works with 100+ Worx tools
cons: Camera struggles in low light — early-morning starts skipped
More expensive than wire-based rivals at the same area
mowers.verdict: The big-lawn cousin of the Landroid Vision M600, and the most ambitious robot mower on UK shelves. When the camera works it's brilliant — the mower stays on the grass, leaves the borders alone, ignores the dog. When it doesn't work, you're rebooting it from your phone at 7am. The Vision platform is genuinely the future of robot mowing, but the L1600 is paying early-adopter tax. Wait for the 2026 hardware refresh if you can.
mowers.tip: Used Vision L1600s appear at £900-1200 — a lot of buyers panic-sell after a frustrating first month. The mower does get better with software updates so a 12-month-old unit performs noticeably better than a brand-new one out of the box. Confirm the camera lens isn't scratched (deal-breaker, £230 to replace) and that the buyer has the original Worx login so warranty transfers.

mowers.tagline: Honda's 56V cordless — the same chassis as the petrol HRG 466
pros: Honda chassis — same plastic deck as the petrol HRG 466
Quiet enough that the neighbours stop complaining
cons: Not self-propelled — surprising for the price
Single 4Ah battery only — about 35 minutes runtime
Honda dealer markup vs. EGO/Greenworks equivalents
mowers.verdict: Honda playing catch-up to EGO. The HRG 466 XB is fine — Honda fit and finish, decent runtime, the warranty Honda owners expect — but at £749 it's £100 more than an EGO LM1701E that has self-drive and a bigger battery. Buy this mower if you already own Honda OPE and want one charger across everything. Otherwise the EGO is the better mower at less money.
mowers.tip: Used HRG 466 XBs are uncommon — Honda owners tend to keep them. Best deals come from Honda dealers selling ex-demo at £450-550 with full warranty transfer. The 56V battery is Honda-specific; aftermarket cells don't exist, so battery health on a used buy is critical. Demand a runtime test — if it doesn't cut for 30 minutes on full charge, walk away.

mowers.tagline: Electric-start 48cm self-propelled petrol — Hyundai's value flagship
cons: Hyundai engine is not Honda — service intervals matter
Plastic wheel hubs wear after 2-3 seasons
Battery for electric start is consumable — £45 replacement
mowers.verdict: The mower that broke the £400 self-propelled-with-electric-start barrier. It does not match a Mountfield SP46 or a Cobra MX46SPB on long-term durability — those are 10-year mowers, this is a 5-year mower — but the price difference is enormous and the spec sheet on paper is identical. For owners who don't service their machines and just want a thing that starts on the button, the Hyundai is the obvious choice.
mowers.tip: Used HYM480SPERs are everywhere on Marketplace at £140-200 because owners panic when the electric-start battery dies after year three. The battery is generic 12V 7Ah — buy any sealed lead-acid from CPC and pay £18. If the seller is dropping the price because 'the electric start is broken', that is your bargain. Confirm pull-cord still works as backup.

pros: Cheapest current petrol push from a known brand
cons: Plastic wheels with bushings, not bearings
Single-lever cutting height adjuster is stiff
mowers.verdict: The Murray EQ400X is the cheapest way into a Briggs-engined petrol mower in the UK. It's not a 10-year machine — the deck is thin steel and the wheels won't last past three seasons of hard use — but at £249 new and £100-130 used it doesn't need to be. For a 600m² lawn that gets cut every fortnight, it's enough mower. The Hyundai HYM430SP is the better buy if you can stretch another £80 for self-drive.
mowers.tip: Used EQ400Xs are a tenner or two cheaper than the equivalent Hyundai because Murray brand recognition is weaker. Briggs 450E should start in two pulls cold, one warm — anything more is a carb service (£25 DIY, £50 trade). Check the deck for rust around the discharge chute; that's where Murrays go first.

cons: Steel deck is thinner gauge than Mountfield
mowers.verdict: The Murray EQ500X is what a self-propelled petrol mower used to cost before Mountfield and Cobra started undercutting each other. Briggs 575EX engine is bulletproof, ReadyStart actually works in the cold, and the 46cm cut is right for most UK lawns. If you don't care about the brand badge, it's better value than the Mountfield SP46 at the same price point.
mowers.tip: Used EQ500Xs at £150-220 are everywhere on Marketplace — Murray is unfashionable so prices stay low. The drive cable is the predictable failure point at 5+ years (£18 part, 30-minute job). Briggs 575EX engines run forever if you change the oil; many used examples have never had an oil change so budget £8 for fresh oil and a filter on day one.

mowers.tagline: 51cm self-propelled petrol with InStart electric — Murray flagship push
pros: InStart push-button electric start — no pull cord
Briggs 675iS is the upgraded 'is' (idle stop) variant
cons: InStart battery is consumable (~£40 every 4 years)
Murray dealer support thin — InStart faults need B&S service centre
mowers.verdict: Murray's biggest petrol push. The InStart electric-start system is genuinely brilliant when it works — press button, mower starts — but the lithium battery pack is a known weak point, and out-of-warranty replacement is expensive enough to write the mower off. Buy new with full warranty or buy used very cheap; there's no middle ground that makes financial sense.
mowers.tip: Used EQ700Xs are scarce because owners run them until the InStart dies, then bin them. £250-330 for a working example is realistic. The fallback pull-cord is fitted but hidden under the cowling — confirm it works before buying. If the InStart battery is dead, the seller usually doesn't know it's a £40 part and will accept £180 — that's the deal.

pros: Briggs Industrial-Commercial (I/C) — uprated bearings and oil bath filter
Same red/yellow heritage paint as the 11/30
cons: Dated steering — heavy at low speed
mowers.verdict: The Murray 14.5/38 was the model up from the 11/30, with the I/C-spec Briggs engine that runs longer between rebuilds and a wider deck for serious paddock work. Out of production for 20+ years but still common on Marketplace because the I/C engine and cast axle outlast everything around them. The mower of choice if you need to cut a half-acre rough lawn cheaply.
mowers.tip: £500-900 used for a tidy example. The I/C engine is the headline — rotate the flywheel by hand and listen for valve clatter (normal) vs. bottom-end knocks (deal-breaker). Cast front axle should have no play at the kingpins. Avoid any with non-original deck spindles or a welded chassis. A clean 14.5/38 will run another 15 years.

mowers.tagline: Current 42in lawn tractor — the modern Murray ride-on
pros: Briggs Intek 7180 V-twin — smoother than single-cylinder rivals
Cheapest V-twin lawn tractor in UK new
cons: Murray UK distribution remains patchy post-B&S restructure
Hydrostatic transaxle is the budget Tuff-Torq, not the K46
Steel deck stamping not as deep as Mountfield 1530H
mowers.verdict: If you want a V-twin lawn tractor and the Mountfield 1530H is £400 out of budget, the Murray MX175-107 is the obvious answer. Same Briggs Intek 7180 engine you'd find on a John Deere E120 at twice the price. The trade-off is the transaxle and the dealer network — both are below Mountfield/John Deere standard, and you'll feel that at the 5-year mark when something breaks.
mowers.tip: Used MX175-107s land at £950-1500 depending on hours. Intek 7180s are durable but expensive to rebuild; under 200 hours is the safe buy. Test the hydrostatic in both directions and check for jerky engagement (fluid change due, £35 fix). Confirm the catcher bag is included — replacement is £180 from B&S spares.

cons: Mains cable is a real-world problem
Plastic deck dents at the first kerb hit
mowers.verdict: The Murray MRD300 is a 'first lawnmower' — a tenant's mower for a small front lawn, or the spare you keep at the holiday cottage. It's not built to last but it costs less than a tank of petrol for a Honda. For a flat lawn under 200m² and no ambition for stripes, it's enough. Anyone with a real garden should look at the Bosch Rotak 32 instead.
mowers.tip: Used MRD300s are £25-50 because the next owner usually upgrades within a year. The induction motor itself almost never fails — the failures are cracked decks and severed mains cables. Bring a 13A test plug and try it on the seller's lawn. If the cable has been spliced or repaired with electrical tape, walk away — that's a fire risk.

cons: AL-KO Pro engine less proven than Honda or Briggs in UK
Parts harder to source than Mountfield
Heavier than a comparable Mountfield SP46
mowers.verdict: A solid mid-tier petrol that punches above its UK profile. The AL-KO Pro engine is competent but you trade Honda-grade resale value for £100 off the upfront price. If you can buy from a UK dealer with service backup, this is a sensible buy.
mowers.tip: Used examples £180-260. Check the BBC drive belt is engaging cleanly. AL-KO parts come from Germany and can take 7-10 days, so ask the seller about local servicing before you commit. Avoid examples with worn-out drive cables — replacement is £40 in parts but fiddly.

mowers.tagline: Cylinder mower for ornamental UK lawns
pros: Cassette system lets you change to scarifier or brush
cons: Cylinder mowers won't cut grass longer than ~50mm
mowers.verdict: If you want a county-cricket-club lawn at home, this is one of two ways to get there (the other is the Allett Liberty 35 cordless). The Stirling 17 is a real cylinder mower with interchangeable cassettes — it sets a tone for the lawn that no rotary can match. Wildly overkill for 99% of UK gardens.
mowers.tip: Used Stirlings are rare and hold value — £700-1100 depending on age and cassette inventory. Check cylinder blade alignment (a feel for it is worth more than a measurement), ask which cassettes are included, and budget £100-150 for a professional regrind every 2-3 years.

mowers.tagline: British ride-on with Powered Grass Collector
pros: Powered Grass Collector is genuinely powerful
Cassette system for sweeper or scarifier
cons: Service intervals every 50 hours add cost
Cordless ride-ons (EGO ZT5200) closing the gap
mowers.verdict: The C-series is what British country buyers buy when they don't want a John Deere. The Honda iGX is a notch above the Briggs in the cheaper rivals and the Powered Grass Collector handles wet British grass better than belt-driven competitors. A genuine 15-year machine.
mowers.tip: Used C40s £2200-3500 depending on age and hours. Always check hours on the dash — under 500 is excellent, over 1500 needs a deep service. Look for the original PGC unit (often missing on second-hand sales). Countax dealers will refurbish older units for £600-900 which restores resale value.

cons: Cub Cadet engine less known in UK than Briggs or Honda
mowers.verdict: Cub Cadet is MTD's American flagship and the XM1 is a solid wide-cut petrol with one foot in commercial territory. The 53cm deck gets through a lawn fast. If you can find a UK dealer with parts on shelf, it's a credible alternative to Mountfield in this segment.
mowers.tip: Used £180-320. UK supply is patchy so used examples are often 2-4 years old at most. Check the deck for cracks — the welds on the front deck stiffener are a known stress point. Don't pay over £350 for a used one with no service history.

pros: Ferrex 40V battery shared with hedge trimmer / strimmer
mowers.verdict: Aldi Specialbuy cordless that lands every March and disappears within two weeks. For a tiny urban lawn under 250m² this is the cheapest way into cordless. Don't expect it to last past 5 years and don't bank on getting a replacement battery when the original dies.
mowers.tip: Used £30-60. Always check the battery still holds charge — Aldi cordless batteries are the weak point. If the seller can't demonstrate a 15-minute runtime, walk away. Don't pay extra for a cracked grass box — they're £18 from Aldi when in season.

pros: Honda GCVx 200 — best-in-class engine for this segment
cons: £999 ceiling — Hayter Harrier alternative at similar price
mowers.verdict: Lawnflite is MTD's UK brand and the 553HRS-PROHW is their premium petrol — Honda engine, ali deck, lifetime deck warranty. A direct rival to the Hayter Harrier 56 at this price and arguably better value if stripes don't matter. Underrated brand.
mowers.tip: Used £350-550. Check the Honda engine first (oil colour, spark plug condition, easy start) — the Lawnflite chassis is bombproof but the engine determines the buy. Ask about the deck warranty: it transfers to new owners if the original receipt is available. Lawnflite parts come through MTD UK and are well-stocked.

pros: £269 for a self-propelled petrol is good value
Loncin engine is competent if uninspiring
cons: Loncin engine is no Honda — expect 7-10 years not 15
mowers.verdict: B&Q's house-brand petrol that does the job for a medium UK lawn at a fair price. The Loncin engine isn't exciting but it starts and runs reliably with annual servicing. Trade up to Mountfield SP46 for £30 more if you want a UK-icon mower that holds resale value.
mowers.tip: £90-160 used. The drive cable is the weak point — if it's stretched, the self-propulsion will be feeble. £18 to replace as DIY. Always check B&Q can still order spares for the model (some Mac Allister generations get end-of-lifed quickly). Avoid examples with cracked deck mounts.

pros: Parkside X20V battery family — shared across 100+ tools
cons: Uses 2x 20V batteries (not 40V single) — half the runtime feels
Parkside not stocked outside Lidl Specialbuy windows
mowers.verdict: If you already own Parkside X20V tools, the PRMA 40-Li is a no-brainer addition that piggybacks on your existing battery investment. Standalone it's a fair 350m² cordless. Watch for the Specialbuy windows (typically March-April and August) when these appear at Lidl.
mowers.tip: Used £45-90. Check both batteries hold charge for at least 10 minutes each — Parkside batteries are individually replaceable at £25-35 from Lidl in-store events. A working pair with a charger is worth £60-80 even without the mower.

pros: 40cm cut is wider than most at this price
cons: 40L grass box fills fast on wider cut
mowers.verdict: Spear & Jackson under £100 with a rear roller is genuinely unusual — Bosch wants £200 for the equivalent. The S1340ER is the budget choice for a small striped lawn near a power point. Tools-of-the-trade quality it isn't, but for £99 you get a working striped finish.
mowers.tip: Used £30-50. The 12-year guarantee is the genuine USP — if the seller has the original purchase receipt, it transfers and that's £100+ of warranty value attached to a £40 mower. Always check that. The plastic roller is the wear part; replacement is £18 direct from S&J.

pros: Personal Pace self-propulsion — pushes when you push
Briggs 163cc engine is a known quantity
cons: American 22" deck is unusual in UK metric world
mowers.verdict: Toro is the dominant American mower brand and the Recycler 22" is their volume model. The Personal Pace system is genuinely clever — speed responds to your walking pace, not a fixed gear. UK supply is patchy but if you can get one, it's an excellent wide-cut petrol.
mowers.tip: £280-450 used. Briggs 163cc parts are everywhere (filter, plug, blade). The Personal Pace belt is the only model-specific service item — £35 part, fiddly to fit, budget £80 if you can't DIY. UK Toro dealers (Lely UK, P Tuckwell) keep parts but expect 5-10 day lead times.

pros: Yard Force 40V battery shared with full garden range
cons: Yard Force less known than EGO or Greenworks
Battery replacement £80 only direct from Yard Force
mowers.verdict: A solid mid-budget cordless from a brand best known for robotic mowers. The 5Ah battery handles a typical 600-700m² UK back garden. Yard Force's 40V system is decent if not class-leading; comparable Greenworks 40V offers a wider tool range.
mowers.tip: Used £110-180. The battery is the make-or-break — anything under 4 minutes hold-charge is dead, anything over 40 minutes runtime is healthy. Yard Force batteries are model-specific (no cross-brand swaps), so a knackered battery means a £80 commitment to replace.

pros: Steel deck — unusual on cordless at this price
cons: £599 ceiling sits awkwardly against LM2135E-SP at £699
mowers.verdict: The sensible Goldilocks EGO. Sized between the entry LM1701 and the flagship LM2135E-SP, the LM2024E-SP is genuinely the right cordless for the typical UK suburban back garden. Steel deck at this price is rare on cordless.
mowers.tip: £300-440 used. EGO holds value better than any other cordless brand — even 4-year-old examples sell for £350+. Insist on the original EGO battery, test runtime (40+ min is healthy), and check the deck for the inevitable kerb dents which don't affect function. The wheels are a wear part — £20 each direct from EGO.

pros: Cordless — no fuel, no oil, no winter prep
EGO 56V batteries shared across the range
Quiet at 78 dB compared to 100 dB petrol equivalent
cons: 8000m² per charge — replace batteries mid-job on bigger plots
Battery replacement £200 each, x4 in 10 years = £800
mowers.verdict: The first credible cordless zero-turn for residential UK buyers. The ZT4204E does what a petrol zero-turn does at a fraction of the running cost and noise. If you have a flat, 5000-8000m² lawn and don't need to collect clippings, this is the future-proof buy. For ride-on plus collector, you still need petrol or a Husqvarna Automower.
mowers.tip: Used market still establishing — £2800-3800 for examples under 100 hours. Check all four batteries individually for capacity. The mowing deck is the wear part long-term; replacement deck blades are £35 each (3 needed). Inspect the zero-turn pivots for play before paying premium.

pros: NERA platform with EPOS satellite navigation option
cons: Boundary wire still required (NERA without EPOS upgrade)
NERA Automower Connect+ subscription for full features
Lawn under 200m² is overkill for the 405X
mowers.verdict: The cheapest way into the NERA generation of Husqvarna robotic mowers. Perfect for small-to-medium urban gardens where the headline is silence and zero effort. Pay extra for the EPOS satellite-navigation module if you want to skip the boundary wire installation.
mowers.tip: £550-800 used for 2-3 year old examples. Always check the battery hours (logged in the app) — a 405X with 1500+ hours is approaching battery replacement (£180). The blades are tiny consumables — £8 for a pack of 9 lasts 6 months. Boundary wire breaks are the most common service call.

mowers.tagline: Husqvarna entry petrol with push start
pros: Husqvarna engine is well-supported in UK
cons: Husqvarna 123cc engine less proven than Honda or Briggs long-term
Plastic deck on a petrol mower
mowers.verdict: Husqvarna's entry petrol — the orange branding gets buyers but the underlying mower is fairly standard. For 400-700m² flat lawns where you don't mind pushing, the LC 141C is light and reliable. Cheaper Mountfield options match it on capability.
mowers.tip: Used £100-180. Check the recoil starter operates smoothly. The Husqvarna 123cc engine accumulates carbon in the exhaust if run on E10 without stabiliser — listen for misfire under load. Service kit (oil, plug, air filter) is £18 from any Husqvarna dealer.

cons: Boundary wire required (no satellite navigation option)
Premium price for capability matched by Husqvarna 305 at lower cost
Stihl battery family less widely shared than Husqvarna
mowers.verdict: Stihl's mid-range robotic — solid hardware with the Stihl dealer support advantage. The RMI 422.2 PC handles up to 800m² well and the app scheduling is straightforward. For the same money the Husqvarna 410 XE NERA gives you satellite navigation upgrade options.
mowers.tip: £480-680 used. Check the cutting disc for chipped blades (£12 for a replacement pack). Always look at the wheel motors — high-time examples have play in the front swivel. Boundary wire installation is the biggest pain — pay £150-200 for a Stihl dealer to do it properly.

pros: Bosch 40V battery shared with newer GardenCare range
cons: Older Bosch 18V tools won't share batteries
mowers.verdict: Bosch is moving its garden range from 18V PowerForAll to a new 40V platform and the CityMower 22 is one of the first models. Cleaner styling and brushless motor make it a step up from the older Rotak 32. For sub-300m² urban back gardens it's a fair £269 buy.
mowers.tip: Used £85-150. The 40V Bosch system is too new for big used supply — check the battery cycles in the included diagnostic via the Bosch app. Make sure the seller is offloading both the battery and charger (Bosch chargers are model-specific to 40V and not cheap separately).

pros: Greenworks 60V battery shared with the Pro range
Smart-Cut motor adjusts to grass density
mowers.verdict: Greenworks 60V Pro is the underdog cordless platform in the UK — slightly cheaper than EGO with comparable performance. The GD60LM46HP is a solid self-propelled 46cm for a typical 1000m² lawn. The smaller dealer network is the trade-off vs EGO.
mowers.tip: Used £180-300. The 60V battery is the part that loses value — anything 3+ years old needs runtime tested. Greenworks support quality varies by retailer (Amazon UK vs Mowdirect vs Robert Dyas). Check the warranty registration before paying for any examples sold within the 5-year window.

pros: Makita LXT 18V battery family — 200+ tools share the same battery
cons: Body-only — batteries and charger sold separately (~£200 extra)
Uses 2 x 18V batteries (twin-battery design) — runtime split
mowers.verdict: For tradespeople already invested in Makita LXT, the DLM533Z is the cordless mower buy — your existing batteries work, the build quality is professional-grade, and the 53cm cut is among the widest on cordless. Residential buyers without an LXT collection should look at EGO instead.
mowers.tip: Used body-only £200-320; kit with 2 batteries + charger £350-480. Always check both batteries match in age (twin-battery design works best with batched batteries). Makita batteries are widely faked on eBay — only buy genuine 6Ah BL1860B (printed marking is sharp, not blurry).

pros: Ryobi One+ battery family — over 150 tools share 18V batteries
cons: Body-only — needs 2 x One+ batteries (£90 extra for genuine 4Ah pair)
mowers.verdict: The Ryobi One+ ecosystem is the broadest 18V battery family on the UK market. If you have Ryobi tools, the RY18LMX40A-0 lawn mower is a £179 addition with no extra battery investment. Without existing One+ batteries, the EGO LM1701 is a better single-mower buy.
mowers.tip: Body-only used £75-120; kit with 2 batteries £140-210. Always test with a known-good 4Ah battery pair — Ryobi One+ batteries are faked on eBay (look for the moulded Ryobi logo and PCB code through the contact-window). Brushless models are RY18 — older brushed RLM18 models are not the same mower.

pros: Mountfield 40V battery shared with hedge trimmer and grass trimmer
cons: Mountfield 40V family is small vs EGO 56V or Greenworks 60V
mowers.verdict: Mountfield extends its dominant UK petrol position into cordless with the Empress range. The 38 Li is a fair entry cordless aimed at owners trading down from a Mountfield petrol when arthritis or noise becomes a problem. Outclassed on cut quality by EGO LM1701 for similar money.
mowers.tip: Used £80-140. The Mountfield 40V battery family is sparse so used examples often miss the original battery and charger — verify both are included and working. Battery replacement is £55-75 direct from Mountfield. Plastic deck checked for cracks at the front lip is the main inspection point.

mowers.tagline: B+D 48cm corded electric with bike handle
pros: 48cm cut is unusually wide for a corded electric
Bike-handle reduces vibration on long sessions
cons: Cable management on bigger lawn is a fight
mowers.verdict: The bigger sibling of the BEMW461BH and one of the widest corded electrics under £200. For 600-900m² flat lawns near a power point, the 48cm cut earns its keep — you finish faster than a 40cm rival. Don't expect Honda longevity but at £169 you don't have to.
mowers.tip: Used £55-95. The 1800W motor is well over-spec for the deck so even neglected examples usually run. Always test the cable end-to-end (some users splice without proper waterproof junction), and check the grass box latch — it cracks on the inner tab. £8 replacement direct from B+D.

cons: Brushed motor is older tech than premium rivals
mowers.verdict: The cheapest way into a cordless Flymo for under-200m² lawns. The brushed motor and Flymo-only battery mean this is an end-of-life-disposable rather than a platform buy. For a flat patch near a courtyard, it does the job at £169.
mowers.tip: £35-70 used. Always check battery holds at least 15 minutes runtime; Flymo Mighti-Mo batteries are £45 to replace and only available direct. The grass box is a known wear part — cracked tabs are common. Avoid examples with deck dents from kerbs.

pros: AIA cut-pattern adapts to lawn shape
cons: Reviews mixed — software stability varies by firmware version
ACS anti-collision sensors are an extra-cost upgrade
Worx app less polished than Husqvarna Automower
mowers.verdict: Worx Landroid is the budget alternative to Husqvarna Automower — cheaper hardware, weaker software, but the PowerShare battery family is a real asset for existing Worx tool owners. For 500m² simple lawns, the M500 Plus is workable. Don't expect it to handle complex multi-zone gardens like a NERA 410 XE will.
mowers.tip: £230-380 used. Firmware version matters more than year — always update before buying decisions are made. Worx wheels are the cheap part (£12 each) and easily replaced. The PowerShare battery slot fits other Worx 20V batteries from your hedge trimmer, which is a genuine perk over Husqvarna proprietary battery design.

pros: Electric start at this price is rare
cons: Hyundai engine less proven long-term than Honda/Briggs
Electric-start battery is a 12V Ah replacement at year 5-6
mowers.verdict: The HYM510SPE undercuts Mountfield by £30-50 and adds electric start, which is the genuine USP. The Hyundai engine is competent if not heroic — expect 7-10 years of reliable use rather than the 15-20 a Honda HRX delivers. A solid value buy if you don't care about stripes.
mowers.tip: £160-260 used. Always check the electric-start battery (small sealed Ah pack under a side panel). A dead battery doesn't kill the mower (recoil still works) but a working button is worth £30. The IC196 takes the same NGK plug and air filter as the Briggs 750EX — spares are cheap and on shelf at any Hyundai or generic small-engine dealer.

mowers.tagline: Bigger T-series petrol lawn tractor, 48in deck
pros: Kawasaki FS600V V-twin, parts on shelf at any small-engine dealer
Hydrostatic drive smooth in both directions
122cm deck cuts an acre in under an hour
Kubota service stamps hold resale strongly
cons: Side-discharge or collection only, no mulch as standard kit
Heavier than the T1880 on soft turf
Kubota dealer network thinner than John Deere
mowers.verdict: The T1880's bigger brother for half-acre lawns and beyond. Same agricultural Kubota build with a wider Kawasaki engine, and the same retained-value story when you sell. Most domestic buyers do not need the extra 20cm of deck, but if your lawn justifies it this is the rational upgrade.
mowers.tip: £3000-4800 used. Kawasaki FS600V hours under 600 are excellent. Test the hydro drive on a real incline, slipping under load is the standard wear point on any Kubota petrol ride-on. Confirm the collection chute is included, side-discharge-only units sometimes turn up cheaper.

mowers.tagline: Legacy Kubota diesel ride-on, heavy-duty spec
pros: Direct predecessor to the G23, same Kubota D722 diesel
HD body and reinforced collector for daily commercial work
Parts shared with the current G-series, still well supported
Used examples are a third of the price of a current G26
cons: Discontinued, no factory warranty on a used unit
Older 3-cylinder lacks the cleaner emissions of the D902
Heavy at 460kg, needs proper barn or trailer storage
mowers.verdict: Discontinued but still working all over the country. Cricket clubs, schools, and head gardeners run G21s with 4000+ hours on them and no end in sight. Buy on hours and service history, not year, and this is the cheapest way into proper Kubota diesel groundskeeping.
mowers.tip: £4500-9000 used depending on hours. Under 1500 hours is excellent for any G21, 3000-4000 is normal and fine if serviced. Always confirm the deck spindles spin freely without grinding, replacement is a five-figure job on the older G-series. Diesel injector spray pattern matters, ask for a recent compression test on anything over 2500 hours.

mowers.tagline: Legacy Kubota diesel ride-on, light-duty configuration
pros: Same D722 diesel as the HD, lower-spec body for domestic use
Lighter than the HD on softer ground
Cheapest route to a Kubota diesel ride-on
Mid-mount mower deck with side discharge
cons: LD body is less rugged for heavy commercial use
Same heavyweight footprint as any G-series
mowers.verdict: The domestic-spec version of the G21. Same engine and same essential mowing performance, with a lighter body that suits estate owners who do not need commercial-grade frame stiffness. On the used market the LD/HD distinction barely affects price, condition matters far more.
mowers.tip: £4000-8500 used. Hours and service stamps dictate price more than LD vs HD. Confirm the rear PTO is operational, some LD units never had the optional PTO fitted from new. Check the brake pedal feel, soft pedal often means master cylinder rebuild.

mowers.tagline: Kubota G23 diesel ride-on, light-duty spec
pros: Same D722 diesel as the HD with a lighter LD frame
Cheaper than the HD by around a grand new
Easier to manoeuvre than the HD on smaller estate lawns
cons: LD frame is fine for domestic use, not for daily commercial
Same diesel maintenance routine as the HD
Resale gap to the HD widens as units age
mowers.verdict: If you want a current G23 but do not need the HD's commercial frame, the LD is the answer. Same Japanese diesel reliability, lower price, and most domestic owners will never notice the missing duty cycle. Cricket clubs and contractors should still buy the HD.
mowers.tip: £5000-8500 used. Hours under 1500 are excellent on any G23. Confirm it is the genuine LD and not a stripped-down HD, the frame stampings are visible behind the seat. Service history matters more than year on any Kubota diesel.

pros: HD frame built for daily commercial use, the contractor's choice
D902 diesel, cleaner-burning than the older D722
600L collection bag, the biggest in the G-series
Electronic dash with hour counter and service warnings
cons: Same heavyweight footprint as the LD
mowers.verdict: The current Kubota G-series flagship in commercial spec. What golf-course head greenkeepers and large grounds contractors actually run on, and the unit Kubota itself uses for demo days. Build quality is the same Japanese standard as their agricultural tractors, with a service horizon measured in decades.
mowers.tip: £7000-12000 used. Genuinely new enough that hours under 800 are common. Confirm the electronic dash is fault-free, Kubota dealer diagnostic is a one-hour bench job and worth £150 of confidence on any unit over 5 years old. D902 service history is non-negotiable, ask for stamped intervals.

mowers.tagline: Entry Kubota front-mount, 4WD commercial out-front rotary
pros: Cheapest way into a Kubota out-front mower
4WD across all four wheels for proper banks and slopes
Out-front deck cuts right up to walls and trees
cons: Petrol, not diesel, in a class where diesel is the norm
Side-discharge or mulch only, no collection box
Mowing speed lower than a comparable Z-series zero-turn
mowers.verdict: The entry F-series for contractors and estates who want Kubota build but cannot stretch to the diesel F3090. Out-front rotary deck is the real benefit, lets you mow under benches and around obstacles in a way no mid-mount can. Vanguard petrol is the trade-off, perfectly fine for under 500 hours a year, less ideal for full commercial routes.
mowers.tip: £3500-7500 used. Vanguard hours under 800 are good. Test the 4WD engagement, the front prop shaft can develop play if greasing was skipped. Confirm the deck height-adjust pedal works smoothly without binding. Out-front belt should look dry and tight, not glazed.

mowers.tagline: Kubota mid-range diesel front-mount, the contractor staple
pros: D1503 diesel, the workhorse engine, 8000-hour service life proven
Optional Kubota PowerFold collector for proper estate spec
Out-front deck plus diesel torque equals serious productivity
cons: Diesel particulate filter on later models adds maintenance complexity
mowers.verdict: The Kubota that local council parks departments and large estates standardise on. Diesel torque means it does not care about wet grass or steep ground, and the D1503 engine will outlast three generations of operator if the oil gets changed. Worth the price gap from the F251 if you actually mow for a living.
mowers.tip: £7500-15500 used. Hours under 2000 are excellent, under 4000 acceptable with full service stamps. Confirm the PowerFold collector if listed, that single accessory is £3000 if bought separately. Hydraulic deck-lift pressure-test on any unit over 3500 hours, deck-lift cylinder seals are the standard wear point.

mowers.tagline: Top Kubota diesel front-mount, 38HP commercial flagship
pros: V1505 4-cylinder diesel, the smoothest engine in the F-range
PowerFold collector option for clean-pickup work
Built for daily 8-hour shifts year after year
cons: Heavy at 920kg, needs a proper trailer to move
Overkill for anything under three acres
mowers.verdict: What sportsturf head groundsmen and large landscape contractors actually buy when the budget is approved. Four-cylinder diesel is creamy smooth, the 183cm deck eats ground, and the optional PowerFold collector finishes the job to display standard. Pure professional kit, retail buyers should look at the F3090.
mowers.tip: £9500-19500 used. V1505 diesels routinely hit 8000 hours, so do not panic at 4000-5000 hours if service stamps are clean. Insist on a deck inspection from underneath, blade spindles are the largest single repair cost on any F-series. Confirm DPF status on post-2014 units, regen-cycle issues are the modern failure mode.

pros: Cheapest way into a Kubota zero-turn, residential price-point
Kawasaki FR651V is reliable and serviceable everywhere
Tight zero-turn radius cuts mowing time roughly in half
Steel deck and tubular frame, not the cheap stamped builds
cons: Side-discharge as standard, mulch kit and collector are extras
Steeper learning curve than a steering-wheel ride-on
Petrol zero-turns chew fuel faster than diesel Z-series
mowers.verdict: For owners of half-acre lawns who want serious mowing speed without committing to a commercial zero-turn. Kawasaki engine and Kubota frame combine into a machine that comfortably out-cuts any conventional ride-on at the same price. Beginners should test-drive before buying, the dual-lever control takes a Saturday to master.
mowers.tip: £1900-3800 used. Kawasaki FR-series hours under 400 are excellent. The single biggest used-market check is the hydro pump bypass levers on the back, owners who used them to push the mower can ruin the pumps, ask before bidding. Deck spindle play is normal under 200 hours, anything over 0.5mm needs new bearings.

pros: Kawasaki FX691V, the pro-grade commercial V-twin
Welded steel deck, not stamped, for daily use
Hydro-Gear ZT-3400 transmissions, the same as commercial Ariens and Wright
cons: Petrol fuel cost on commercial routes
No suspension seat as standard at this price
mowers.verdict: The Kubota zero-turn that small landscape contractors actually run. Pro Kawasaki engine, commercial-grade hydro pumps, and the same deck weld quality as machines costing 50 per cent more. Diesel commercial buyers should look at the ZD series, everyone else gets the right balance of price and durability here.
mowers.tip: £3500-7000 used. Kawasaki FX hours under 800 are excellent, under 2000 acceptable. Hydro pumps are the standard wear point on any zero-turn, listen for whining under load. Deck-belt tension is operator-adjustable, a stretched belt is a £40 fix, not a deal-breaker.

mowers.tagline: Top Kubota petrol zero-turn, 60in pro commercial
pros: Kawasaki FX801V, the top commercial petrol V-twin in production
Hydro-Gear ZT-5400 transmissions for daily 8-hour use
cons: Heavier than the Z421 on soft ground
Diesel ZD1211 covers similar acreage for similar money
mowers.verdict: The largest petrol zero-turn Kubota sell in the UK and the natural choice for landscape contractors who do not want to commit to diesel servicing. Kawasaki FX801V is reckoned to be the smoothest of the FX line, the deck is genuinely commercial, and the suspension seat earns its keep after the first 3-hour run. Worth a hard look against the ZD1211 if your routes justify diesel.
mowers.tip: £5000-9000 used. FX801V hours under 1200 are excellent for this class. Confirm the seat air-suspension is functional, replacements are £400. Deck weld inspection from underneath is essential on any zero-turn over £6000, look for cracks at the spindle mounts.

pros: D1105 diesel, low-fuel-burn engine, 6000-hour service horizon
Welded commercial deck with replaceable cutting chamber
Properly built for daily commercial routes
Kubota long-engine warranty on the diesel
cons: Diesel-zero-turn money, eye-watering for domestic buyers
Heavier than the petrol Z-series on soft turf
mowers.verdict: The cheapest Kubota diesel zero-turn and the most-bought ZD in the UK contractor market. D1105 diesel torque means it cuts wet grass without bogging, and the 48in deck still fits through standard estate gates. The diesel premium pays back inside two seasons of commercial use, less for domestic owners.
mowers.tip: £5500-10500 used. ZD1011 hours under 1500 are excellent, under 3000 normal and acceptable with stamps. D1105 oil pressure spec matters, low-pressure warning on a warm engine is a £2000+ repair. Hydro-pump bypass levers on the back, check for evidence of being pushed in neutral.

pros: D1305 diesel, the natural step up from the ZD1011
Pro-grade Parker hydro pumps with relief valves
cons: Heavier than the ZD1011 on banks and slopes
DPF on post-emissions units adds service complexity
mowers.verdict: The ZD that most UK landscape contractors actually scale up to. Bigger deck, bigger fuel tank, bigger duty cycle, and the same Kubota diesel reliability. Two acres per hour all-day pace is realistic on this machine, and parts availability through the Kubota dealer network is the best of any commercial zero-turn brand in the UK.
mowers.tip: £7000-14500 used. D1305 hours under 2000 excellent. Always check the deck spindles from underneath, the 60in deck has three spindles instead of two, more wear points to inspect. Confirm regen-cycle history if post-2015 emissions unit, blocked DPF is a £1500+ dealer job.

mowers.tagline: Top Kubota diesel zero-turn, 72in pro commercial
pros: V1505 4-cylinder, the same engine as the F3890 front-mount
183cm deck genuinely covers commercial scale ground
Hydraulic deck lift, no foot pedal effort
Pro-grade Parker pumps and reservoirs for full-day duty
cons: Council-and-contractor pricing, well into commercial territory
Heavy at 640kg, marks lawns more than smaller ZDs
DPF service adds to running costs
mowers.verdict: The Kubota ZD flagship for grounds contractors, sports-turf teams, and golf-club mowing fleets. Smooth 4-cylinder diesel, hydraulic-lift 72in deck, and a hydraulic-system spec that genuinely supports 8-hour days season after season. Domestic buyers should not even read the price, pros will recognise it as fair for what it does.
mowers.tip: £10500-18500 used. V1505 hours under 2500 are excellent for this class. Hydraulic deck lift cylinders seal-fail under 4000 hours, a £400 dealer job, ask about service history. ROPS frame must be original Kubota with intact certification stickers for resale value.

mowers.tagline: Mid Kubota sub-compact tractor with 60in mid-mount mower
pros: Real sub-compact tractor first, mower second, takes front loader and rear implements
D902 diesel, the modern emissions-clean Kubota 3-cylinder
Permanent 4WD, ideal for sloping lawns and estate roads
Mid-mount deck cuts at proper out-front-grade quality
cons: Tractor-first design, mowing is one job of many
Properly heavy at 580kg, marks soft turf in wet weather
Mid-mount deck is an extra-cost option in some packages
mowers.verdict: The middle BX-series, sized for smallholdings and large estates who need a tractor that also mows. Better all-rounder than a pure ride-on if you also need to plough snow, move logs, or grade a track. Pure-mowing buyers should look at the G or T series instead.
mowers.tip: £6500-9500 used for mower-configured units. Confirm the mid-mount deck is included, often sold separately new and routinely missing from used trade-ins. D902 hours under 1500 are excellent for a domestic-use BX. Front-loader frame brackets should be present even without the loader, they add £600 to resale.

mowers.tagline: Top BX sub-compact tractor with 60in mid-mount mower
pros: Top BX-series, 26HP from the same D902 family
Same 4WD chassis as the BX2380, more pulling power for implements
Takes the full Kubota implement range, loader, backhoe, snow blade
Mid-mount deck cuts to commercial-grade finish
cons: Top-BX pricing, well into compact-tractor money
Heavy enough to need proper turf consideration in wet weather
Overkill for owners who only mow
mowers.verdict: The full Kubota sub-compact, for smallholdings and large estates that need a single machine to mow, clear, load, and grade. Same mid-mount mower deck as the BX2380 with extra engine power for serious implement work. If you do not need the loader and backhoe capability, the BX2380 saves around £2000 with no real cut-quality difference.
mowers.tip: £7500-11500 used for mower-configured units. Front loader presence adds £2500-4000 to private sale price, factor that into negotiation. D902 hours under 2000 are typical for a BX2680 used on a working smallholding. Hydraulic-system service history is non-negotiable, three-point linkage and mid-PTO share the same circuit.

categories.name: Petrol
categories.desc: Best for medium-to-large lawns and rough conditions. No cord, no battery limits. The category that still dominates UK gardens over 800m².

categories.name: Electric
categories.desc: Corded mains-powered mowers. Quiet, reliable, low maintenance — but tethered. Right for small-to-medium lawns near a power point.

categories.name: Cordless
categories.desc: Battery-powered. Quiet and clean. Battery runtime is the deciding factor. The fastest-growing category in UK lawncare.

categories.name: Hover
categories.desc: Air-cushioned, no wheels. Brilliant on slopes and odd-shaped lawns. The British answer to awkward gardens.

categories.name: Robotic
categories.desc: Set it and forget it. Boundary-wire and wire-free RTK robots are mature; camera-based no-wire systems are improving fast.

categories.name: Ride-On
categories.desc: For gardens that take more than an hour to push-mow. A serious purchase. Engine hours matter more than years.

categories.name: Manual
categories.desc: Push-reel cylinder mowers. Silent, zero emissions, surprisingly satisfying. Best for small ornamental lawns.

brands.name: Honda
brands.focus: Premium petrol mowers with class-leading engines
brands.ukPosition: Top-tier — sets the benchmark for petrol reliability
brands.blurb: Honda's lawnmowers carry the same engineering ethos as their cars: over-engineered, quiet, and built to outlast you.

brands.name: Mountfield
brands.focus: Mid-market petrol and ride-on mowers, popular UK choice
brands.ukPosition: Best-selling UK lawnmower brand by volume
brands.blurb: Italian-engineered, UK-distributed. Mountfield dominates British garden centres for good reason — solid kit at fair prices.

brands.name: Hayter
brands.ukPosition: Iconic British brand for striped lawns
brands.blurb: British-built since 1946. Hayters are the choice of cricket-club groundsmen and people who care deeply about their stripes.

brands.name: Stihl
brands.focus: Petrol, cordless, and the iMOW robotic line
brands.blurb: German engineering with the strongest UK dealer network. Stihl AP cordless and AK home tools share batteries cleanly.

brands.name: Cobra
brands.blurb: The British distributor's house brand. Cobra punches above its weight on price-to-feature, especially the rear-roller petrol mowers.

brands.name: Webb
brands.blurb: Webb has been making mowers in Birmingham for over a century. Today the badge sits on entry-level petrol and hand-push cylinders.

brands.name: Hyundai
brands.blurb: Korean badge, generic engines. Hyundai mowers offer surprisingly wide cuts and steel decks at sub-Mountfield prices.

brands.name: Stiga
brands.blurb: Italian parent of Mountfield and Atco. Stiga-branded mowers tend to feel a notch above their stablemates.

brands.name: Bosch
brands.ukPosition: Trusted electric range, strong DIY following
brands.blurb: German precision applied to home-and-garden. Bosch's Rotak and CityMower lines are go-tos for small UK lawns.

brands.name: Flymo
brands.blurb: The brand that invented the hover mower. Flymo is in millions of British sheds — disposable but capable.

brands.name: Einhell
brands.blurb: German DIY brand with a single-battery cordless ecosystem (Power X-Change). Solid value, growing UK presence.

brands.name: EGO
brands.blurb: EGO killed the 'cordless can't replace petrol' argument. The 56V XP series matches a Honda HRX on cut quality without fumes.

brands.name: Husqvarna
brands.blurb: Swedish heritage. The Automower line invented the robotic mower category and still leads it on durability.

brands.name: Greenworks
brands.blurb: Cordless-only brand with strong 60V Pro line. Genuinely competitive with EGO at a slightly lower price.

brands.name: Ryobi
brands.blurb: If you already own Ryobi 18V tools, the ONE+ mowers slot in beautifully. The 36V flagship competes with EGO.

brands.name: Makita
brands.blurb: Japanese trade-grade. LXT batteries hold value better than any rival platform — that alone is worth the premium.

brands.name: Worx
brands.ukPosition: Highly popular in the robotic market
brands.blurb: Worx made the boldest move in robotics with the camera-based Landroid Vision. PowerShare 20V tools cross-compatible.

brands.name: Gardena
brands.blurb: German garden brand under Husqvarna. PowerForAll battery shared with Bosch home tools — useful crossover.

brands.name: Mammotion
brands.focus: Wire-free RTK and AWD robotic mowers
brands.blurb: Chinese newcomer that has Husqvarna nervous. AWD on slopes is a genuine breakthrough. UK support still scaling.

brands.name: Segway
brands.blurb: Segway took longer than Husqvarna to get the basics right but the Navimow line is now reliable and well-supported.

brands.name: John Deere
brands.ukPosition: Default sensible ride-on for half-acre+ UK lawns
brands.blurb: American agricultural icon. JD ride-ons hold value remarkably well — spend slightly more, save it back on resale.

brands.name: Cub Cadet
brands.blurb: American heavy-duty ride-on. Kohler engine is the highlight; UK service network is patchy.

brands.name: Countax
brands.blurb: British-built since 1989. Countax tractors with Powered Grass Collectors handle wet UK grass like nothing else.

brands.name: Atco
brands.blurb: Heritage British brand from 1921. The Liner roller petrol is a Marketplace classic — heavy steel deck, proper stripes.

brands.name: Qualcast
brands.focus: Budget electric, petrol, and Suffolk Punch cylinder
brands.blurb: Argos/Homebase legacy brand. The Suffolk Punch is the cylinder mower bowling-green keepers still hunt for on Marketplace.

brands.name: Mac Allister
brands.blurb: B&Q's house brand. Performs above price thanks to Briggs engines, but build quality is below Mountfield.

brands.name: Black+Decker
brands.blurb: The default Argos electric for 15 years. Pure utility purchase — fine, not heirloom.

brands.name: Parkside
brands.blurb: Lidl Middle Aisle special with a genuine cult following. The 20V battery slots into a wide Parkside cordless ecosystem.

brands.name: Ferrex
brands.blurb: Aldi's answer to Lidl Parkside. The 40V cordless punches above sub-£200 rivals when the Specialbuy lands.

brands.name: Spear & Jackson
brands.blurb: Argos/Homebase budget line. The 1800W corded with rear roller is a genuine bargain on the Argos shelf.

brands.name: Westwood
brands.blurb: British-built sister brand to Countax — same factory, same engineering, slightly cheaper. PGC blower handles wet grass.

brands.name: Yard Force
brands.ukPosition: High volume on Amazon and Argos
brands.blurb: High-volume robotic and cordless on Argos and Amazon. Reasonable starter robot; not a longevity rival to Husqvarna.

brands.name: Toro
brands.focus: American premium walk-behind and Flex-Force cordless
brands.ukPosition: Heritage US brand with growing UK presence
brands.blurb: American pedigree from one of the largest commercial-mower makers in the world. Toro's Flex-Force 60V cordless line is genuinely competitive with EGO; UK distribution is still thinner than US.

brands.name: Allett
brands.ukPosition: Premier ornamental-lawn brand in the UK
brands.blurb: British family-owned since the 1960s. Allett are the cylinder mower brand cricket clubs and ornamental gardeners actually buy. Liberty cordless brought their pedigree into the battery age — interchangeable cassettes (cylinder, scarifier, brush) make a single deck do four jobs.

brands.name: AL-KO
brands.focus: German petrol and cordless — solid mid-tier
brands.ukPosition: Strong in DE/AT/CH, growing UK dealer network
brands.blurb: German engineering applied to petrol and cordless lawn mowers. Stronger presence in continental Europe than the UK, but UK dealers are growing — particularly in northern England and Scotland.

brands.name: Murray
brands.focus: American ride-on tractors — heritage value brand
brands.ukPosition: Heritage US brand, common on UK Marketplace as second-hand
brands.blurb: Murray has been making ride-on tractors in the US since the 1920s. The red-and-yellow striped 11/30 of the 1990s is a Marketplace classic — built simple, repaired easily, parts cheap. Modern Murray tractors continue under Briggs & Stratton ownership.

brands.name: Kubota
brands.focus: Japanese diesel ride-on tractors and compact utility
brands.blurb: Japanese agricultural giant. Kubota garden tractors are typically diesel, properly built, and serviceable for 20+ years. Used by professional groundsmen, landed estates, and golf clubs. Domestic buyers stop at the price; everyone else buys nothing else.

brands.name: Lawnflite
brands.blurb: Lawnflite is MTD's UK brand and one of the most underrated names in British mowing. Honda-engined walk-behinds with cast aluminium decks and a lifetime deck warranty undercut Hayter on price for similar capability — the trade-off is fewer people recognising the name.
